track.name: AI Engineering
track.description: Ship LLM-powered features end-to-end: prompts, RAG, agents, evaluation, and production integration.

rubric: Knows how to force reliable structured output from an LLM.
body: You need an LLM to return data your code can parse every time. Which approach is the most reliable?

options.text: Ask nicely in the prompt to 'return JSON' and hope for the best
Use function-calling / a structured-output schema and validate the result against it
Raise the temperature so the model is more creative with formatting
Parse the response with a regular expression after the fact

payload.explanation: A declared schema (function-calling / structured outputs) plus validation is the robust way to guarantee parseable output; prompt-only requests drift.

rubric: Can reason about prompt design and iteration.
body: Walk me through how you'd design and iterate on a prompt for a feature that summarizes support tickets into a fixed set of fields. How would you know it's good enough to ship?

evaluation_criteria: Starts from the desired output shape / fields and works backward to the prompt
Mentions few-shot examples, constraints, and handling edge cases (empty/ambiguous tickets)
Defines a concrete 'good enough' bar (a small labelled set, target accuracy) rather than eyeballing
Describes iterating on failures rather than one-shot prompting

rubric: Understands when to use system vs user roles and few-shot.
body: Where should stable, reusable instructions (tone, output rules, guardrails) live in a chat-completions call?

options.text: Repeated inside every user message
In the system message
In the model's temperature setting
In the function name

payload.explanation: Durable behaviour belongs in the system message; user messages carry the per-request content.

rubric: Understands what embeddings enable in retrieval.
body: In a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system, what is the primary purpose of embeddings?

options.text: To compress the model's weights for faster inference
To represent text as vectors so semantically similar chunks can be found by nearest-neighbour search
To encrypt the documents before storage
To translate the documents into English

payload.explanation: Embeddings map text into a vector space where distance approximates semantic similarity, powering retrieval.

rubric: Can design an end-to-end RAG pipeline and name its failure modes.
body: Design a RAG pipeline that answers questions over a company's internal docs. Describe the stages, and tell me two ways it can return wrong answers and how you'd mitigate them.

evaluation_criteria: Names the core stages: chunk → embed → store → retrieve top-k → augment prompt → generate
Identifies failure modes (bad chunking, low-recall retrieval, hallucination beyond context)
Proposes concrete mitigations (chunk overlap, re-ranking, citing sources, grounding instructions)
Mentions how retrieval quality is measured, not just asserted

body: In a tool-using agent, what does the model actually return when it decides to use a tool?

options.text: The final answer directly — tools are ignored at runtime
A structured tool call (name + arguments) that your code executes, then feeds the result back to the model
The source code of the tool it wants to run
A probability distribution over all tools that you average

payload.explanation: The model emits a tool name + arguments; the application runs the tool and returns the observation so the model can continue.

rubric: Can implement a simple tool-dispatch step.
body: Implement `dispatch(tool_name, args)` that routes to the right tool. Support 'add' (returns args['a'] + args['b']) and 'upper' (returns args['text'].upper()). For any unknown tool, return the string 'unknown tool'.

payload.expected_approach: Dictionary or if/elif dispatch on tool_name; default branch returns 'unknown tool'.

rubric: Understands how to keep multi-step agents reliable.
body: A multi-step agent sometimes loops forever or calls the wrong tool. What controls would you add to keep it reliable in production?

evaluation_criteria: Mentions a step/iteration budget or max-tool-calls to prevent infinite loops
Discusses validating tool arguments and handling tool errors gracefully
Mentions logging/tracing each step for debugging
Considers a fallback or 'give up' path when the agent is stuck

rubric: Knows the point of a regression eval.
body: You changed a prompt to fix one bad case. What is the main purpose of running your eval set afterwards?

options.text: To make the model faster
To catch regressions — cases that used to pass but now fail because of the change
To reduce the API bill
To retrain the base model

payload.explanation: An eval/regression set verifies a fix didn't silently break previously-passing cases.

rubric: Can stand up an evaluation pipeline for an LLM feature.
body: How would you build an evaluation pipeline for an LLM feature where the 'right' answer isn't a single exact string? Talk about the eval set, scoring, and how it plugs into your workflow.

evaluation_criteria: Builds a representative labelled eval set covering normal + edge cases
Chooses appropriate scoring (exact-match where possible, rubric/LLM-as-judge or similarity for open-ended)
Runs it automatically (CI / before deploy) and tracks a score over time
Explains acting on failures — triaging and adding new failures back into the set

rubric: Can read eval metrics and decide whether to ship.
body: A new prompt version raised overall accuracy from 82% to 86%, but on the 'refunds' category it dropped from 90% to 71%. Should you ship it, and what would you do next?

expected_insights: Notices the overall gain hides a serious per-category regression on refunds
Concludes you should NOT ship as-is (or ship gated) because a key category got worse
Proposes drilling into the refunds failures and fixing before rollout
Values per-segment metrics over a single headline number
payload.data: Overall accuracy 82% → 86%. Per-category: refunds 90% → 71%, billing 80% → 88%, general 79% → 85%.

rubric: Solid Python: parse and transform JSON-like data.
body: Write `top_model(usage)` that takes a list of dicts like {'model': 'gpt', 'tokens': 100} and returns the model name with the highest total tokens across the list. If the list is empty, return None.

payload.expected_approach: Aggregate tokens per model in a dict, then return the key with the max value; guard the empty case.

rubric: Understands why async matters for API-bound work.
body: You need to call an LLM API 50 times independently. Why is asyncio (or concurrent requests) a big win here versus a plain for-loop?

options.text: It makes each individual API response return faster
The calls are I/O-bound, so overlapping the waiting means total time approaches the slowest call, not the sum
It uses multiple CPU cores to speed up the model
It reduces the number of tokens each call uses

payload.explanation: API calls are I/O-bound; concurrency overlaps the idle wait time so total latency is bounded by the slowest call rather than their sum.

rubric: Can reason about the latency/cost/quality trade-off with real metrics.
body: A feature works but is too slow and too expensive in production. Without hurting quality much, what levers would you pull, and how would you decide which ones actually helped?

evaluation_criteria: Names concrete levers (smaller/cheaper model for easy cases, caching, shorter prompts/context, batching, streaming)
Ties decisions to measured latency + cost per request, not guesses
Guards quality with the eval set while optimizing
Prioritises by impact rather than changing everything at once

rubric: Knows what to instrument for LLM observability.
body: Which set of signals is most useful to log for observability of an LLM feature in production?

options.text: Only the final response text
Prompt, response, token counts, latency, cost, model version, and errors — per request
Just the total number of requests per day
The developer's local console output

payload.explanation: Rich per-request traces (inputs, outputs, tokens, latency, cost, version, errors) are what let you debug quality, cost, and regressions.
